ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for lion's mane mushroom cheesy cutlet rice specifically. FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (composite rice-based dish) used as closest parent. No specific code exists for this fusion mushroom cutlet rice preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for this specific fusion dish. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared composite restaurant dishes at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate comprising approximately 150 g quinoa multi-grain rice, one lion's mane mushroom cheesy cutlet (~120 g), a portion of french fries (~80 g), mixed salad (~60 g), and brown sauce (~40 g), totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Mushroom, lion's mane, raw (FDC ID 2346405); Cooked quinoa (FDC ID 168917); Breaded fried vegetable cutlet analogue; Cheddar cheese (FDC ID 173414); French fries (FDC ID 170720); Mixed salad greens (FDC ID 169248); Brown gravy sauce (FDC ID 173580)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct nutritional entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Values are estimated by weighted-average composition of the identified components: lion's mane mushroom cutlet (breaded, fried) ~27%, quinoa multi-grain rice ~33%, french fries ~18%, mixed salad ~13%, brown sauce ~9%. Macros derived from USDA FoodData Central individual ingredient entries and combined proportionally. Energy cross-checked using Atwater factors (4/9/4 kcal per g protein/fat/carbohydrate).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 2021;114(5):1625–1632. Analogous entries: cooked quinoa GI ~53, mixed grain rice GI ~48–55, breaded fried foods GI ~50–55.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being quinoa multi-grain rice (GI ~53 per University of Sydney GI Database) and breaded fried components (GI ~50–55). Weighted estimate accounts for the mixed grain base (quinoa lowers GI relative to white rice). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (52 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 52.7, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 22.5 × 4.5 = 101.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per full serving) from brown sauce, cheese, and breaded cutlet
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that a full serving of this dish may contribute approximately 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg; portion reduction or requesting sauce on the side is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~52.7 per serving) driven by quinoa multi-grain rice and breaded cutlet carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: The quinoa multi-grain rice base provides a lower GI (estimated ~52) compared to white rice, which is beneficial for postprandial glucose management, but the large carbohydrate load per full serving (~101 g) warrants portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and frying oil (~2.1 g per 100 g; ~9.5 g per serving) and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat from cheese and fried components and high sodium per serving represents a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lion's Mane Mushroom Cheesy Cutlet Rice is a global fusion dish served at Flavours by Saute (Funan, Singapore). It features a breaded and pan-fried lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) cutlet coated with melted cheese, served alongside quinoa multi-grain rice, french fries, a mixed salad, and brown sauce. The dish is marketed as an 'East meets West' concept, combining the umami-rich, seafood-like texture of lion's mane mushroom — a functional mushroom prized in East Asian cuisine — with Western-style breaded cutlet preparation and cheese. The quinoa multi-grain rice base provides a lower glycaemic index (~52) compared to white rice, contributing dietary fibre and plant protein. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan due to the cheese and egg binder in the cutlet coating. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 168 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 6.8 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate. A full plate (~450 g) delivers approximately 756 kcal and ~101 g carbohydrate, making portion awareness important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. Sodium content is moderate-to-high (~1395 mg per serving) due to the brown sauce and cheese. Lion's mane mushroom is noted in emerging research for potential neuroprotective and immunomodulatory properties, though these are not clinically established at typical dietary intake levels.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-average composition of identified components using USDA FoodData Central individual ingredient entries. GI is estimated from analogous quinoa multi-grain rice entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the dish composition is clearly described.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data; no direct HPB Singapore Foodo or equivalent primary source entry was found for this specific dish. 2. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore restaurant plate composition for a rice-with-sides meal; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources and lion's mane mushroom vitamin D content is highly variable depending on UV exposure during cultivation; not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry used. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on quinoa multi-grain rice (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database) as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 5. Halal status is listed as false because the restaurant (Flavours by Saute) is not confirmed halal-certified; the dish itself contains no pork or alcohol but certification status is unverified. 6. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional spelling 'Lion Mane Cheeeezy Cutlet Rice' to the canonical English form 'Lion's Mane Mushroom Cheesy Cutlet Rice'. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are approximate. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for this composite estimate.
